column32: B76F Mary Creek, Ukalunda Beds, Ukalunda District, North Queensland
column43: Ukalunda Beds in Mary Creek, ""Hidden Valley"", 8 miles NNE of Mt. Wyatt, Ukalunda district, southwestern part of the Bowen 1:250,000 Sheet area, north Queensland
column105: the authors state, "the age of the fauna would appear to be within the range Siegenian to Givetian, with Couvinian as the favoured probability"; later the statement is also made that "as in previous papers on Australian coral faunas by Hill (e.g. 1950), we use the sequence in the south of the Dinant Basin as our standard of reference for the Devonian, so that we subdivide the Middle Devonian into the Couvinian and Givetian stages, rather that Eifelian and Givetian stages"
column106: the present collections are from two stratigraphic units: the ukalunda beds and conglomerate at the base of the overlying drummond group.  the corals in the latter are found in limestone pebbles in the conglomerate and it is assumed that the pebbles were derived from an older limestone.
column169: the present collections are from two stratigraphic units: the ukalunda beds and conglomerate at the base of the overlying drummond group.  the corals in the latter are found in limestone pebbles in the conglomerate and it is assumed that the pebbles were derived from an older limestone.

column32: Eromanga Basin L165
column43: Western side of Tully Range (Brighton Downs 1:250 000 map)
column105: The Winton Formation has yielded spore-pollen assemblages belonging to the Phimopollenites pannosus or succeeding Appendicisporties distocarinatus zone of probable late Albian to Cenomanian age (Dettmann & Playford 1969; Burger, 1970; Morgan, 1980; Helby et al., 1987). Moore & Pitt (1984), on the basis of unpublished palynological data, suggested that the uppermost beds of this formation in central parts of the basin may have been deposited in the late Cenomanian or early Turonian.
column106: Plant fossils are preserved as impressions in buff, pale yellow, dark brown, or pink, siltstones or fine-grained lithic sandstones. Small cross laminae and parallel laminae are evident in the host rocks.
column119: Small cross laminae and parallel laminae are evident in the host rocks and together with the relatively undamaged leaf remains and numerous slender invertebrate (?oligochaete) trails, they suggest relatively low enery, ?lacustrine depostional environments.
column140: Leaves are relatively undamaged
column149: Illustrated specimens are located in either the Queensland Museum (QMF) or the National Museum of Victoria (MNVP)

column6: Wintonopus latomorum n. gen. n. sp.
column32: Lark Quarry dinosaur tracksite (QM)
column43: Lark Quarry, between the townships of Winton and Jundah in west-central Queensland; about 95 km SW of Winton. Now within Lark Quarry Conservation Park.
column105: upper part of formation
column106: "Sediments exposed at Lark Quarry are mainly soft and fine-grained arkosic sandstones of reddish-buff colour. The sandstones for thick beds (each built of innumerable small sets of cross-bedding) and are interlayered with thin and persistent seams of indurated pinkish claystone. The dinosaur tracks are impressed in an 8-10 cm seam of finely laminated claystone that is evidently of lacustrine origin...There are no ripple-marks, rain-prints or sun-cracks, and the dinosaur tracks were impressed in firm moist mud."
column119: lacustrine origin. "Sandstones above and below the footprint bed probably resulted from periodic sheet-floods...Shrinkage of the lake had exposed extensive mud-flats marked with narrow run-off channels."
column140: thought to represent a stampede of small ornithopods and theropods following the approach of a large "carnosaurian" theropod
column149: more than 130 individual dinosaurs

column32: Eromanga Basin L406
column43: 6 km northwest of Whyrallah Homesead (Mackunda 1:250 000 map)
column105: The Winton Formation has yielded spore-pollen assemblages belonging to the Phimopollenites pannosus or succeeding Appendicisporties distocarinatus zone of probable late Albian to Cenomanian age (Dettmann & Playford 1969; Burger, 1970; Morgan, 1980; Helby et al., 1987). Moore & Pitt (1984), on the basis of unpublished palynological data, suggested that the uppermost beds of this formation in central parts of the basin may have been deposited in the late Cenomanian or early Turonian.
column106: Plant fossils are preserved as impressions in buff, pale yellow, dark brown, or pink, siltstones or fine-grained lithic sandstones. Small cross laminae and parallel laminae are evident in the host rocks.
column119: Small cross laminae and parallel laminae are evident in the host rocks and together with the relatively undamaged leaf remains and numerous slender invertebrate (?oligochaete) trails, they suggest relatively low enery, ?lacustrine depostional environments.
column140: Leaves are relatively undamaged
column149: Illustrated specimens are located in either the Queensland Museum (QMF) or the National Museum of Victoria (MNVP)
column169: Plant fossils are preserved as impressions in buff, pale yellow, dark brown, or pink, siltstones or fine-grained lithic sandstones. Small cross laminae and parallel laminae are evident in the host rocks.

column32: JCUL154, Top Hut Lst Mbr, Quinton Fmn, Broken River Province, NE Queensland
column43: All collection are from the north of the Graveyard Creek Subprovince, NE Queensland. This collection: W bank of Gray Creek, 500 m upstream from Mistake Creek junction; general collection from thinly bedded limestone near Top Hut Outstation
column105: Allochthonous limestones near the base of the Quinton Formation. Stratigraphic sequence of members is not clear. Age assignment based on graptolites.
column106: Siliciclastic sediments form the bulk of the Quinton Formation. Corals are from allochthonous limestone blocks. "The Top Hut Lst...consists of thinly interbedded, irregular to almost nodular limestone and rarer shale, and is host to a rich conodont and invertebrate fauna...The Lst is a yello-brown to dark brown crinoidal biomicrite, which has been partially to fully recrystallized. Bedding ranges from 1-2 cm to 1 or 2 m, and is strongly contorted in places; this contortion probably occurred during downslope slumping of the block."
column119: "The limestones occur within predominantly siliciclastic turbiditic sediments, and consist of large slumped blocks, calcirudites and calcarenites. These were probably derived from contemporaneous shallow marine areas along the unstable N margin of a small, rapidly subsiding basin, which developed during the Early Silurian in a probably extensional tectonic setting."
column150: Most of the fauna is not well studied. This monograph is confined to rugose corals.
column169: Siliciclastic sediments form the bulk of the Quinton Formation. Corals are from allochthonous limestone blocks. column105: Allochthonous limestones near the base of the Quinton Formation. Stratigraphic sequence of members is not clear. Age assignment based on graptolites.
column106: Siliciclastic sediments form the bulk of the Quinton Formation. Corals are from allochthonous limestone blocks. "The Top Hut Lst...consists of thinly interbedded, irregular to almost nodular limestone and rarer shale, and is host to a rich conodont and invertebrate fauna...The Lst is a yello-brown to dark brown crinoidal biomicrite, which has been partially to fully recrystallized. Bedding ranges from 1-2 cm to 1 or 2 m, and is strongly contorted in places; this contortion probably occurred during downslope slumping of the block."\r\nComments on geology:
column119: "The limestones occur within predominantly siliciclastic turbiditic sediments, and consist of large slumped blocks, calcirudites and calcarenites. These were probably derived from contemporaneous shallow marine areas along the unstable N margin of a small, rapidly subsiding basin, which developed during the Early Silurian in a probably extensional tectonic setting."
column150: Most of the fauna is not well studied. This monograph is confined to rugose corals.\r\n
column169: Siliciclastic sediments form the bulk of the Quinton Formation. Corals are from allochthonous limestone blocks. column32: UQL4847, Tomcat Creek Lst Mbr, Quinton Fmn, Broken River Province, NE Queensland
column43: All collections are from the north of the Graveyard Creek Subprovince, NE Queensland. This coll: Near top of W slope of hill, 250m E of Tomcat Creek channel, 1650m earing 152deg (magnetic) from junction of Tomcat Creek with Gray Creek; thinly bedded limestone immediately to W of massive limestone exposure
column105: Allochthonous limestones near the base of the Quinton Formation. Stratigraphic sequence of members is not clear. Age assignment based on graptolites.
column106: Siliciclastic sediments form the bulk of the Quinton Formation. Corals are from allochthonous limestone blocks. Gray Creek Member: "The limestones...contain coral and conodont faunas, and are discontinuously exposed for about 250m long strike within a thick sequence of shale, greywacke, conglomerate and minor calcirudite. These limestones may have been emplaced by large scale slumping or may be channel deposits within the turbidite sequence as suggested by Sloan et al. (1995). The most prominent Lst is a dark grey, massive, recrystallized biomicrite which reaches a max. thickness of about 35m, and which is poor in macrofossils. [Collected localities consist of] poorly exposed, thinly bedded limestone which has yielded a relatively rich rugose coral fauna..."
column119: "The limestones occur within predominantly siliciclastic turbiditic sediments, and consist of large slumped blocks, calcirudites and calcarenites. The most prominent Lst is a dark grey, massive, recrystallized biomicrite which reaches a max. thickness of about 35m, and which is poor in macrofossils. [Collected localities consist of] poorly exposed, thinly bedded limestone which has yielded a relatively rich rugose coral fauna..."

column105: Age call based on conodont Polygnathus perbonus found from base and 111.6 m above base of section HID (which has collections going up to 144 m).\r\nJA: originally entered as Emsian; the perbonus zone is early Emsian
column106: Ukalunda Beds include siltstone, sandstone, limestone, and calcareous shale. Localities B220 and B610F occur in dark brown to khaki, highly jointed calcareous siltstone. column32: Locality UQL2953, Shield Creek Fmn, N Queensland
column43: All localities on the E slope of the main NNE-SSW ridge 6-7.3 km N of Old Pandanus Creek Homestead, 40 km SW of Greenvale and about 200km NW of Charters Towers, N Queensland.
column105: Formation of Lochkovian/Pragian age. Collections from upper part, but not entirely clear from this statement whether they are Pragian. The Carlinastraea fauna spans both stages.
column106: "The material is from...a number of limestone lenses outcropping on the E slopes of the quartzite ridge running N from 'Pandanus Creek' homestead...Stratigraphically, the lenses occur in the middle 50m of the Shield Creek Formation which in this area is c. 160m thick and predominantly of medium to coarse arkose with thin mudstone interbeds and thin, clean, very coarse quartz-arenite lenses. The limestone lenses consist predominantly of thickly bedded calcarenites and calcirudites composed mainly of fragments of algae, stromatoporoids, corals, brachiopods, crinoid debris and other shelly material, and intraclasts, all set in a fine micritic matrix.
column119: Some evidence in some collections that corals were rolled and transported.
column150: Rugose corals only. See lithologic description for other taxa known (as fragments) but not studied.

column32: Chinaman Creek Lst Lower Fauna, Broken River Province, N Queensland
column105: Late Emsian to early Eifelian
column106: Ref. 10847: "2250 feet of limestone with moderate-sized bioherms at the base" "Predominantly detrital limestones with thin calcareous shales and several thick coarse sandstone beds; large reefal limestones in basal unit...Interbedded in the Broken River Formation and grading laterally into muddy sediments to the south"

column32: Locality UQL3574, Shield Creek Fmn, N Queensland
column43: All localities on the E slope of the main NNE-SSW ridge 6-7.3 km N of Old Pandanus Creek Homestead, 40 km SW of Greenvale and about 200km NW of Charters Towers, N Queensland.

column106: "The material is from...a number of limestone lenses outcropping on the E slopes of the quartzite ridge running N from 'Pandanus Creek' homestead...Stratigraphically, the lenses occur in the middle 50m of the Shield Creek Formation which in this area is c. 160m thick and predominantly of medium to coarse arkose with thin mudstone interbeds and thin, clean, very coarse quartz-arenite lenses. column43: outcrop(s) at Bluff Downs station, along the banks of Allingham River, a tributary of the Burdekin River, north Queensland
column105: early Pliocene according to Becker et al. (2008)\r\nK/Ar dating of the basalts capping the Allingham Formation at its type section (Bluff Downs flow) reveal a minimum age of 3.6 Ma (see Mackness et al., 2000, Austr. J. Earth Sci. 47).
column106: Mackness et al. (2000) briefly describe the fossiliferous sequence within the Allingham Fm. as clays silts, sands and calcareous sands. This sequence is overlain by a nodular limestone called the Chara Limestone which in turn is overlain by basalt. Below the erosive ase of the Allingham Fm. a lateritic horizon occurs in which channels are cut down filled with basalt pebble-bearing deposits. This implies that the Allingham Fm. in its entirety is coeval with the basaltic volcanism in that area and that basalt flows probably dammed streams and thus created the conditions under which the Allingham Fm. was deposited. The basalt flows including the Allingham Fm. are referred to as Nulla Basalt Province the oldest rocks of which are dated as 5.2 Ma. (see Mackness et al., 2000, and references therein).

column32: Birralee Hill
column43: 3 miles ENE of Birralee Hill, Outcrop 464 of D.E.A. Webb Collection
column105: Glendoo Sandstone thought to be lateral equivalent of the middle part of the Gebbie Formation, most likely belonging to the upper Echinalosia maxwelli, E. davidi, or lower E. discinia brachiopod zones of Briggs (1998). These parts of the zones are correlated with the APP3.3 palynomorph zone. Laurie et al. (2016) dated zircons from the Rowan Formation in the Sydney Basin (in the underlying upper Echinalosia preovalis zone, or upper APP3.2) as 271.60 and 271.89 Ma, constraining its upper part to Roadian, given a Roadian-Wordian boundary around 266.9 Ma (Wu et al., 2020). Strata in Western Australia assigned to APP4.1 yielded U-Pb ages of 269.25 Ma or younger, constraining the upper boundary of APP3.3 to the late Roadian (Laurie et al. 2016). As a result, APP3.3 appears to fall entirely within the Roadian.

column32: BMR B1570, Parrot Creek, Mulgrave Yards
column105: Presence of Pseudostrophalosia clarkei suggests an age within the P. clarkei zone, correlated with the upper part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016).
column149: Repository: Commonwealth Palaeontology Collection

column32: BMR B1634, Collinsville
column105: The stratigraphic position within the formation is unknown. Most of the Blenheim Formation is Wuchiapingian in age, containing brachiopod zones correlated with the upper part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone or lower part of APP5 (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age and APP5 as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian. However, the Moonlight Sandstone member contains brachiopod zones correlated with APP4.2 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.2 zone was constrained as late Wordian to mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). In the Sydney Basin, these species are common in the Broughton Formation, dated as older than early Capitanian (a sample 2 m below the top yielded a radiometric date of 262.51 Ma in Metcalf et al., 2015).

column32: BMR B261e, Nebo Synclinorium
column43: Nebo Synclinorium. 20°44'30"S 147°51'00" E.  BOWEN[amg] 5995 7063
column105: Below Scottville Member, from interval between approx. 15.5 m and approx. 32.5 m above base of formation in BMR section 261. Presence of Pseudostrophalosia blakei and Terrakea exmoorensis suggests an age within the P. blakei zone, correlated with the lower part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016).

column32: BMR B270a, Exmoor Homestead
column43: Nebo Synclinorium. BOWEN[amg] near 6188 6835.
column105: Upper Blenheim, close to "Streptorhynchus bed" Shown approximately 400 m above the Scottville Member, and 520 m below the top of the Middle Bowen Beds (i.e. top of Blenheim Formation) in section 5 near Exmoor Homestead. Presence of Echinalosia deari suggests an age within the E. deari zone, correlated with the middle part of APP5 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP5 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian.

However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian.

column32: Bluff Downs station, Main Quarry
column43: outcrop(s) at Bluff Downs station, along the banks of Allingham River, a tributary of the Burdekin River, north Queensland
column105: early Pliocene according to Becker et al. (2008)\r\nK/Ar dating of the basalts capping the Allingham Formation at its type section (Bluff Downs flow) reveal a minimum age of 3.6 Ma (see Mackness et al., 2000, Austr. J. Earth Sci. 47).
column106: Mackness et al. (2000) briefly describe the fossiliferous sequence within the Allingham Fm. as clays silts, sands and calcareous sands. This sequence is overlain by a nodular limestone called the Chara Limestone which in turn is overlain by basalt. Below the erosive ase of the Allingham Fm. a lateritic horizon occurs in which channels are cut down filled with basalt pebble-bearing deposits. This implies that the Allingham Fm. in its entirety is coeval with the basaltic volcanism in that area and that basalt flows probably dammed streams and thus created the conditions under which the Allingham Fm. was deposited. The basalt flows including the Allingham Fm. are referred to as Nulla Basalt Province the oldest rocks of which are dated as 5.2 Ma. (see Mackness et al., 2000, and references therein).

column32: BMR B1572, Parrot Creek, Mulgrave Road Crossing
column43: Also listed as CB1572
column105: 500 feet below top of Middle Bowen Marine Series, from the upper part of the Blenheim Subgroup (Dickins 1989). This part of the Blenheim Formation is Wuchiapingian in age, containing brachiopod zones correlated with the upper part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone or - more likely for this collection - lower part of APP5 (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age and APP5 as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian.

column32: BMR MC293, Blenheim
column105: According to the stratigraphic column in Dickins et al. (1964), MC293 is from near the top of the Blenheim Formation, in "Fauna IV" of Dickins et al. and well above the Big Strophalosia Zone. The Big Strophalosia Zone contains the Pseudostrophalosia clarkei zone of Briggs (1998), correlated with the top of APP4.3 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). Therefore, this locality is likely at a higher level, probably within APP5 palynomorph zone. The APP5 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian.

column32: Yambore Creek, near Maxwelton
column43: The plesiosaur skull (QM F11050) was excavated from the Lower Cretaceous Toolebuc Formation of Yambore Creek, north of Maxwelton in central-northern Queensland
column105: thought to be from the Toolebuc Formation (Rolling Downs Group), Eromanga Basin, latest mid to late Albian P. ludbrookiae zone/upper C. paradoxa-P. pannosus zone (Moore et al., 1986; McMinn and Burger, 1986).\r\nAssignment of QMF11050 to a specific stratigraphic horizon is problematic (owing to limited sediment/invertebrate macrofossil sampling at the type locality), although a broad age range (derived primarily from palynological data) can be established based upon the rock unit of origin. For example, the Toolebuc Formation is placed within the latest mid to late Albian Pseudoceratium ludbrookiae dinoflagellate zone, and the upper Coptospora paradoxa spore-pollen zone in the southern Eromanga Basin (Moore et al., 1986; Alexander and Sansome, 1996), and diachronously in the late Albian Phimopollenites pannosus spore-pollen zone farther north (McMinn and Burger, 1986). Considering that the Maxwelton area falls within the northern extremity of the Toolebuc Formation, a probable Late Albian age for QM F11050 seems likely.

column32: GSQ L421, Stockton 27
column43: MT COOLON[yd] 655 355.
column105: Presence of Echinalosia warwicki suggests the E. warwicki zone, correlated with the lower part of the APP3.1 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP3.1 zone does not have any radiometric constraints, although the top of the overlying APP3.2 is around 271.6 Ma (Laurie et al., 2016), placing it near the Kungurian-Roadian boundary or in the lower Roadian. Phillips et al. (2018) place APP3.1 in the Kungurian, which is consistent with the presence of the ammonoid Neocrimites in APP3.1 of the the Tiverton Formation (although not unequivocal).

column105: Presence of Terrakea brachythaera suggests an age within the E. runnegari/E. wassi zone, correlated with APP4.2 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.2 zone was constrained as late Wordian to mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). In the Sydney Basin, these species are common in the Broughton Formation, dated as older than early Capitanian (a sample 2 m below the top yielded a radiometric date of 262.51 Ma in Metcalf et al., 2015). column32: UQ L4633, Exmoor Homestead
column43: Rock bench nearly 2 m high east of extensive flat, NNE of Exmoor Homestead, east side of Bowen River.  Grid reference 168/855
column105: From beds equivalent to the Glendoo Member of the Collinsville Coal Measures. Gebbie Formation assigned to the Echinalosia maxwelli, E. davidi, and E. discinia brachiopod zones of Briggs (1998). These zones are correlated with the APP3.3 palynomorph zone and APP4.1 for the upper E. discinia zone. Laurie et al. (2016) dated zircons from the Rowan Formation in the Sydney Basin (in the underlying upper Echinalosia preovalis zone, or upper APP3.2) as 271.60 and 271.89 Ma, constraining its upper part to Roadian, given a Roadian-Wordian boundary around 266.9 Ma (Wu et al., 2020). Strata in Western Australia assigned to APP4.1 yielded U-Pb ages of 269.25 Ma or younger, constraining the upper boundary of APP3.3 to the late Roadian (Laurie et al. 2016). As a result, APP3.3 appears to fall entirely within the Roadian. APP4.1 may extend into the Wordian, but a Roadian age is assigned to the formation.

column32: UQ L4637, Bowen River, Exmoor Homestead
column43: North turn of creek east of Bowen River opposite Exmoor Homestead.  BOWEN[amg] 168 828
column105: Stratigraphic position within Moonlight Sandstone unknown. However, the Moonlight Sandstone typically contains brachiopod zones correlated with APP4.2 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.2 zone was constrained as late Wordian to mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). In the Sydney Basin, the Moonlight Sandstone brachiopod species are common in the Broughton Formation, dated as older than early Capitanian (a sample 2 m below the top yielded a radiometric date of 262.51 Ma in Metcalf et al., 2015). As a result, a Wordian-Capitanian age is assigned here.

column32: UQ L4647, Bowen River, Exmoor Homestead
column43: NW side of creek E of Bowen River opposite Exmoor Homestead. BOWEN[amg] 187 828.  There is also an UQ L4647 locality at Gebbie Creek.
column105: Stratigraphic position within Moonlight Sandstone unknown. However, the Moonlight Sandstone typically contains brachiopod zones correlated with APP4.2 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.2 zone was constrained as late Wordian to mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). In the Sydney Basin, the Moonlight Sandstone brachiopod species are common in the Broughton Formation, dated as older than early Capitanian (a sample 2 m below the top yielded a radiometric date of 262.51 Ma in Metcalf et al., 2015). As a result, a Wordian-Capitanian age is assigned here.

column32: UQ L3719, Exmoor Homestead
column43: About 2 km north of Exmoor Homestead. Grid ref 1686/8530.
column105: 150 m stratigraphically above base of Wall sandstone. From beds equivalent to the Glendoo Member of the Collinsville Coal Measures. Gebbie Formation assigned to the Echinalosia maxwelli, E. davidi, and E. discinia brachiopod zones of Briggs (1998). These zones are correlated with the APP3.3 palynomorph zone and APP4.1 for the upper E. discinia zone. Laurie et al. (2016) dated zircons from the Rowan Formation in the Sydney Basin (in the underlying upper Echinalosia preovalis zone, or upper APP3.2) as 271.60 and 271.89 Ma, constraining its upper part to Roadian, given a Roadian-Wordian boundary around 266.9 Ma (Wu et al., 2020). Strata in Western Australia assigned to APP4.1 yielded U-Pb ages of 269.25 Ma or younger, constraining the upper boundary of APP3.3 to the late Roadian (Laurie et al. 2016). As a result, APP3.3 appears to fall entirely within the Roadian. APP4.1 may extend into the Wordian, but a Roadian age is assigned to the formation.

column105: 150 m stratigraphically above base of Wall sandstone. From beds equivalent to the Glendoo Member of the Collinsville Coal Measures. Gebbie Formation assigned to the Echinalosia maxwelli, E. davidi, and E. discinia brachiopod zones of Briggs (1998). These zones are correlated with the APP3.3 palynomorph zone and APP4.1 for the upper E. discinia zone. Laurie et al. (2016) dated zircons from the Rowan Formation in the Sydney Basin (in the underlying upper Echinalosia preovalis zone, or upper APP3.2) as 271.60 and 271.89 Ma, constraining its upper part to Roadian, given a Roadian-Wordian boundary around 266.9 Ma (Wu et al., 2020). Strata in Western Australia assigned to APP4.1 yielded U-Pb ages of 269.25 Ma or younger, constraining the upper boundary of APP3.3 to the late Roadian (Laurie et al. 2016). As a result, APP3.3 appears to fall entirely within the Roadian. APP4.1 may extend into the Wordian, but a Roadian age is assigned to the formation.

column32: Hazelwood Creek, Eungella Station
UQ L1363, Rosella Creek, Havilah
column105: Streptorhynchus pelicanensis bed, according to Campbell (1960, 1965). The pelicanensis fauna occurs near the top of the Blenheim Formation, and is likely equivalent to the Pseudostrophalosia ingelarensis zone (or possibly also P. clarkei zone) of Briggs (1998). These zones are correlated with the upper part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone or lower part of APP5 (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age and APP5 as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian.

column105: Glendoo Sandstone thought to be lateral equivalent of the middle part of the Gebbie Formation, most likely belonging to the upper Echinalosia maxwelli, E. davidi, or lower E. discinia brachiopod zones of Briggs (1998). These parts of the zones are correlated with the APP3.3 palynomorph zone. Laurie et al. (2016) dated zircons from the Rowan Formation in the Sydney Basin (in the underlying upper Echinalosia preovalis zone, or upper APP3.2) as 271.60 and 271.89 Ma, constraining its upper part to Roadian, given a Roadian-Wordian boundary around 266.9 Ma (Wu et al., 2020). Strata in Western Australia assigned to APP4.1 yielded U-Pb ages of 269.25 Ma or younger, constraining the upper boundary of APP3.3 to the late Roadian (Laurie et al. 2016). As a result, APP3.3 appears to fall entirely within the Roadian.

column105: Equivalent of S. pelicanensis bed and Pelican Creek fauna. column32: Coral Creek, below Sonoma road-crossing
column105: The stratigraphic position within the formation is unknown, and is possibly not even from the Blenheim Formation. Most of the Blenheim Formation is Wuchiapingian in age, containing brachiopod zones correlated with the upper part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone or lower part of APP5 (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age and APP5 as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian. However, the Moonlight Sandstone member contains brachiopod zones correlated with APP4.2 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.2 zone was constrained as late Wordian to mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). In the Sydney Basin, these species are common in the Broughton Formation, dated as older than early Capitanian (a sample 2 m below the top yielded a radiometric date of 262.51 Ma in Metcalf et al., 2015).

Most of the Blenheim Formation is Wuchiapingian in age, containing brachiopod zones correlated with the upper part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone or lower part of APP5 (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age and APP5 as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian. However, the Moonlight Sandstone member contains brachiopod zones correlated with APP4.2 palynomorph zone (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.2 zone was constrained as late Wordian to mid-Wuchiapingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). In the Sydney Basin, these species are common in the Broughton Formation, dated as older than early Capitanian (a sample 2 m below the top yielded a radiometric date of 262.51 Ma in Metcalf et al., 2015).

column105: early Pliocene according to Becker et al. (2008)\r\nK/Ar dating of the basalts capping the Allingham Formation at its type section (Bluff Downs flow) reveal a minimum age of 3.6 Ma (see Mackness et al., 2000, Austr. J. Earth Sci. 47).
column106: Mackness et al. (2000) briefly describe the fossiliferous sequence within the Allingham Fm. as clays silts, sands and calcareous sands. This sequence is overlain by a nodular limestone called the Chara Limestone which in turn is overlain by basalt. Below the erosive ase of the Allingham Fm. a lateritic horizon occurs in which channels are cut down filled with basalt pebble-bearing deposits. This implies that the Allingham Fm. in its entirety is coeval with the basaltic volcanism in that area and that basalt flows probably dammed streams and thus created the conditions under which the Allingham Fm. was deposited. The basalt flows including the Allingham Fm. are referred to as Nulla Basalt Province the oldest rocks of which are dated as 5.2 Ma. (see Mackness et al., 2000, and references therein).

column105: Glendoo Sandstone thought to be lateral equivalent of the middle part of the Gebbie Formation, most likely belonging to the upper Echinalosia maxwelli, E. davidi, or lower E. discinia brachiopod zones of Briggs (1998). These parts of the zones are correlated with the APP3.3 palynomorph zone. Laurie et al. (2016) dated zircons from the Rowan Formation in the Sydney Basin (in the underlying upper Echinalosia preovalis zone, or upper APP3.2) as 271.60 and 271.89 Ma, constraining its upper part to Roadian, given a Roadian-Wordian boundary around 266.9 Ma (Wu et al., 2020). Strata in Western Australia assigned to APP4.1 yielded U-Pb ages of 269.25 Ma or younger, constraining the upper boundary of APP3.3 to the late Roadian (Laurie et al. 2016). As a result, APP3.3 appears to fall entirely within the Roadian.

column43: on a sheep station ca. 70 km NE of Winton; in the "Elliot/Mary sector" of the "Elliot Site"
column105: pollen zone inferred from nearby localities\r\n\r\nTucker et al. 2013 and Tucker et al. 2016 detrital zircon analysis suggest a maximum depositional age of 94–92Ma for the Winton Formation. column32: Section SHID, Mary Creek, Ukalunda Beds
column43: Near Mary Creek, Old Hidden Valley, NE of Ukalunda (basis of coordinates). South of section HID.
column105: Age call based on conodont Polygnathus perbonus found from base and 111.6 m above base of section HID (which has collections going up to 144 m).\r\nJA: originally entered as Emsian; the perbonus zone is early Emsian
column106: Ukalunda Beds include siltstone, sandstone, limestone, and calcareous shale. Localities B220 and B610F occur in dark brown to khaki, highly jointed calcareous siltstone. Other Ukalunda spot localities not listed in detail. Douglas Creek siltstone (locality CL172) is grey to brown, well bedded, micaceous siltstone overlying the Douglas Creek limestones. The SHID section consists of olive-grey, thin-bedded, cherty siltstone and medium to coarse lithic arenite.

column32: Wyandotte Creek (Unit A)
column105: The base of the Wyandotte Formation is beyond the conventional 14C range (>45000 ybp). It is inferred to postdate a 410 ka basalt flow. The rate of downcutting to form the valley is not known, but since 410,000 ybp a substantial valley has been cut. Between 4 to 10 metres of Unit A were deposited prior to unit B, which has a basal date of 30,400 ybp (+750/-700 yrs). It is reasonable to suppose that the erosive phase might easily have taken half of the available interval, a corollary of which is that the base of the Wyandotte Formation may not be much older than 200,000 ybp. The duration of the hiatus between final deposition of Unit A and the onset of Unit B is unknown.

column32: New Quarry dinosaur tracksite (QM)
column43: close to Lark Quarry, between the townships of Winton and Jundah in west-central Queensland; about 95 km SW of Winton. Located on "one of a series of small hillside exposures scattered from 100 to about 120 m due S of Lark Quarry."
column105: "about the same stratigraphic level as Lark Quarry" with two track-bearing layers.
column106: "Sediments exposed at Lark Quarry are mainly soft and fine-grained arkosic sandstones of reddish-buff colour. The sandstones for thick beds (each built of innumerable small sets of cross-bedding) and are interlayered with thin and persistent seams of indurated pinkish claystone. The dinosaur tracks are impressed in an 8-10 cm seam of finely laminated claystone that is evidently of lacustrine origin...There are no ripple-marks, rain-prints or sun-cracks, and the dinosaur tracks were impressed in firm moist mud."
column119: lacustrine origin. "Sandstones above and below the footprint bed probably resulted from periodic sheet-floods...Shrinkage of the lake had exposed extensive mud-flats marked with narrow run-off channels.". Supposedly a "much-trampled watering-place."

The sandstones for thick beds (each built of innumerable small sets of cross-bedding) and are interlayered with thin and persistent seams of indurated pinkish claystone. The dinosaur tracks are impressed in an 8-10 cm seam of finely laminated claystone that is evidently of lacustrine origin...There are no ripple-marks, rain-prints or sun-cracks, and the dinosaur tracks were impressed in firm moist mud."

column32: Wyandotte Creek (float specimens)
column105: The base of the Wyandotte Formation is beyond the conventional 14C range (>45000 ybp). It is inferred to postdate a 410 ka basalt flow. The rate of downcutting to form the valley is not known, but since 410,000 ybp a substantial valley has been cut. Between 4 to 10 metres of Unit A were deposited prior to unit B, which has a basal date of 30,400 ybp (+750/-700 yrs). It is reasonable to suppose that the erosive phase might easily have taken half of the available interval, a corollary of which is that the base of the Wyandotte Formation may not be much older than 200,000 ybp. The duration of the hiatus between final deposition of Unit A and the onset of Unit B is unknown.

column43: outcrop(s) at Bluff Downs station, along the banks of Allingham River, a tributary of the Burdekin River, north Queensland
column105: early Pliocene according to Becker et al. (2008)\r\nK/Ar dating of the basalts capping the Allingham Formation at its type section (Bluff Downs flow) reveal a minimum age of 3.6 Ma (see Mackness et al., 2000, Austr. J. Earth Sci. 47).
column106: Mackness et al. (2000) briefly describe the fossiliferous sequence within the Allingham Fm. as clays silts, sands and calcareous sands. This sequence is overlain by a nodular limestone called the Chara Limestone which in turn is overlain by basalt. Below the erosive ase of the Allingham Fm. a lateritic horizon occurs in which channels are cut down filled with basalt pebble-bearing deposits. This implies that the Allingham Fm. in its entirety is coeval with the basaltic volcanism in that area and that basalt flows probably dammed streams and thus created the conditions under which the Allingham Fm. was deposited. The basalt flows including the Allingham Fm. are referred to as Nulla Basalt Province the oldest rocks of which are dated as 5.2 Ma. (see Mackness et al., 2000, and references therein).

Below the erosive ase of the Allingham Fm. a lateritic horizon occurs in which channels are cut down filled with basalt pebble-bearing deposits. This implies that the Allingham Fm. in its entirety is coeval with the basaltic volcanism in that area and that basalt flows probably dammed streams and thus created the conditions under which the Allingham Fm. was deposited. The basalt flows including the Allingham Fm. are referred to as Nulla Basalt Province the oldest rocks of which are dated as 5.2 Ma. (see Mackness et al., 2000, and references therein).

column105: From the upper part of the stratigraphy containing the Big Strophalosia zone and clarkei bed. Upper part of the Blenheim Formation correlated with the upper part of APP4.3 palynomorph zone or lower part of APP5 (Briggs, 1998). The APP4.3 zone was constrained as mid-Wuchiapingian age and APP5 as mid-Wuchiapingian through Changhsingian age by U-Pb dating in Laurie et al. (2016). However, overlying units in the Fort Cooper Coal Measures yield latest Wuchiapingian U-Pb ages (Phillips et al., 2018), constraining the upper Blenheim Fm to Wuchiapingian.

column105: early Pliocene according to Becker et al. (2008)\r\nK/Ar dating of the basalts capping the Allingham Formation at its type section (Bluff Downs flow) reveal a minimum age of 3.6 Ma (see Mackness et al., 2000, Austr. J. Earth Sci. 47).
column106: Mackness et al. (2000) briefly describe the fossiliferous sequence within the Allingham Fm. as clays silts, sands and calcareous sands. This sequence is overlain by a nodular limestone called the Chara Limestone which in turn is overlain by basalt. Below the erosive ase of the Allingham Fm. a lateritic horizon occurs in which channels are cut down filled with basalt pebble-bearing deposits. This implies that the Allingham Fm. in its entirety is coeval with the basaltic volcanism in that area and that basalt flows probably dammed streams and thus created the conditions under which the Allingham Fm. was deposited. The basalt flows including the Allingham Fm. are referred to as Nulla Basalt Province the oldest rocks of which are dated as 5.2 Ma. (see Mackness et al., 2000, and references therein).

column105: Young (2004): A ' Wurungulepis-Atlantidosteus fauna', of assumed Eifelian age, was listed in the macrovertebrate zonation of Young (1993, 1995, 1996). However De Pomeroy (1995: 480) assigned Wurungulepis to the late Emsian serotinus Conodont Zone (CZ), citing a personal communication of J.A. Talent. This information was repeated by Turner et al. (2000: 498). Later (pers. comm. 28/8/95) J.A. Talent had advised A. Basden that this specimen was collected from the grid reference cited above, situated on a bend of the Broken River in an anticline, in strata which were pre-Dosey Limestone in the sequence, and equivalent to the Bracteata Formation and Lomandra Limestone (spanning the Emsian-Eifelian boundary; Sloan et al. 1995 : fig. 3).
